speaker: interviewer
text: Matilda Klein.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I became a [NON-ENGLISH]. And the new transport came in, and we have to have discipline with the girls. And I was screaming at them and telling them. But they were so hungry. They didn't eat for three days, so they were unmanageable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the blockalteste gave 10 sticks for the 10 girls, and she says, now, this will work now. Use them. This will keep discipline with the girls. And I didn't want to take the stick, and I said, then you are out. This was the special privileges, and I was out. That's it. And, basically, special privileges, special treatment, was food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Clothes, too. Some girls were dressed very nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be very nicely dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in style. Some aprons, black aprons. If she had a black apron and dressed, then we knew that she has a special privilege from the Germans. But what privileges? I had no idea. Wasn't interested. And they took the pretty ones. And without hair, I wasn't pretty at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did hair grow back while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did. Yes, it did. It did. It was very small and thick. Yeah, it grow back, and we were very proud of ourselves, how the hair-- we were combing the hair half a day. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for hair to grow back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never cut them again? Just that one time when you came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them got cut again. No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything special happen to you during the time that you were in the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lager?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the A lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why were you moved to the B2 lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just rounded up, and, let's walk over, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far were the two lagers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was 20 minutes walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, did people have a chance to go from one lager to another, or one block to another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Block to another, yes. Lager, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were the blocks from each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like next door house, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends or relationships with anybody in other blocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In A lager, no. In B lager, I had a very good friend, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you got to B lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing like in the A. They just-- I became a [NON-ENGLISH] for two days, and I couldn't take it, and I'm very proud of myself. I survived anyway. And I had a very good friend, Anna. She became sick of typhoid fever-- how you say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhoid fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid fever. They took her in the hospital. And I wanted to find out where in hospital she is. So I was knocking on the wall and calling her name, and I found her. And we were communicating through the wall, and I wasn't supposed to. She was in quarantine. Because it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Contagious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I didn't care. I told her, come to the door. And I went to the door, and I kissed her. And then, I was standing once, talking to her, and I saw the hospital from the main lager was on the same row. They were throwing out dead people from the hospital. They had a wagon with two wheels, and they were keeping the man by the hand and by the legs, and on to the wagon. One on top of the other. And I was crying my eyes out, and this was the end of my crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she remained in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, because I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the B2 lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three months. And again, did you work there at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which lager did you get a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the B. In the B2.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your number, and could we photograph it, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you get it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I can get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did the tattooing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish girls, yeah. It's nothing to it. It's just like a pen. They put it in ink, and they made little dots. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody-- not want-- what happened if you didn't want to have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no such a thing, not want it. Our spirit was broken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When you were on cell appel, were you called by name or by number?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't called at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just stood.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just stood, and they were counting, five and five and five. That's it. No, we weren't called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything of any special event happen to you in the B lager, B2?

speaker: interviewee
text: A baby was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: A baby was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We hear the screams at night. A woman gave birth to a child. We didn't know what the screams are. And in the morning, the blockalteste made a joke to the German woman who was counting that we have one surplus-- that's how you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Surplus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took the baby away to the crematorium, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who gave birth to the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew the woman, yeah. I never knew she's pregnant, because, first of all, she wore a very wide dress, and she never walked alone. She always was surrounded with her relatives, so they wouldn't see that she has a stomach. Yeah, but she survived, then. I don't know if she's alive, but they didn't took her away, because she walked out for cell appel in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: In B2 lager, were you still all Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made friends. I made friends, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from the B2 lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came in civilian man, wearing black, very black.

speaker: interviewer
text: German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, German civilians. And they were selecting people for their factories, for their ammunition. Probably, they were the owners of the factories. And they were selecting groups of girls to work for their factories, and I was selected.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they select them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just counted down. When we were in the cell appel, they counted down, let's say, 300 girls. We need 300 workers, and let's go. That's it, and we went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get-- where did you go, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: To cattle cars for three days, again. We didn't know where we are going. We had no idea. But when we get off, it was Taucha by Leipzig. Taucha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taucha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: T-A--

speaker: interviewer
text: C-H-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: By Leipzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of greeting did you get there? What did you see when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had better treatment. First of all, they had barracks, wooden barracks. Every girl got a cot, separately. And we got a-- a cover, a pillow. And once a day, we had a thick soup to eat, and around two or three slices bread a day, and black coffee in the morning and black in the evening. And for this, we worked 12 hours day shift, and 12 hours night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you working at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory's name I forgot, but the ammunition was a Panzerfaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Panzer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Panzerfaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a piece of pipe around a meter long, and then the faust was like a ball. And it gave just one shot. This one faust gave a shot for 250 meters. Just one shot. And we were-- from the beginning, the whole ammunition was done from raw material until it was finished, except we didn't put in the powder, the gun powder. The Germans did it themselves. And I worked montage. That called the place montage, where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To put together the little parts.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the pipe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you work? Where was the factory itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory was not where the camp. We had to walk every day to the factory. It was a half an hour walk. So we woke up, and 6 o'clock, work starts, and 6 o'clock it finished. So 6 o'clock, we had to be at the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to wake up 4 o'clock in the morning and wash up. We had to be very careful not to get lice, so we had to wash every single day. And once a week, the dress. Because who got lice went to the crematorium. So if you washed your-- how many dresses did you have, one?

speaker: interviewee
text: One.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the dress was being washed, what did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. And where did you wash the dresses?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cold water where we washed ourselves. It was a washroom with a lot of faucets with cold water, and that's it. But we had soap from the factory. Soda, it was very strong, and this kept us out to have lice. And this was interesting, in the morning we had cell appel. We get our black coffee, and then we walked to the factory. We had wooden shoes. We were forced to sing on the way, going and coming back, Germans songs. We were forced to sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marching songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. But I heard on the radio a song that was similar to this, and I get-- I was shuddering. I couldn't take it. I don't remember the songs. It was three songs. We had to sing. But now this is the interesting part. We walked through a town where German people were living. They had small houses, very clean, very neat. They have a garden. They had a fence. The fence, beautiful white. Dog houses, the dogs. And never, ever, a person was outside. And we walked through twice a day for six months. But probably, they weren't allowed to. There never somebody was in the window looking out. But they lived there. I know they lived there, because everything was very clean and very neat. And the population wasn't allowed to look at us. And we worked in the factory with Italian men.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were prisoners, but they weren't with us, and they had much better treatment than we did. They looked better, and better clothes, different clothes. They had, like, black ponchos they were wearing. And we got on our dresses, on the back, a red cross, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: If we run away, so they would recognize us. And we had a coat. And we took out the lining from the coat and put it around here and on the head, from the lining.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was raining. It was cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold there, while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cold, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the factory? Who supervised your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men, women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. Foremans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did, but just to work. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of them ever helpful, nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Maybe they were. But I-- there were girls, they got privileged. I know. But what they did, or what they gave, I have no idea. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you know they were privileged?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a little meat on their bones. They had food, and they was better dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- what kind of food were you served, and who served it? You ate in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought the food into the factory, and they served-- we had a dining room in the factory. And we stand in line, and everybody had a bowl, and they gave you a thick soup, one liter. It was good. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in, already, when? In 1945?

speaker: interviewer
text: '44. And you were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there all winter? Until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware that the war was sort of coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. Planes were coming in, bombarding, and we had to hide. But we couldn't hide in the bunkers. They had bunkers for themselves. We just had to stay inside. That's it. And the work stopped. And when we were at home in the barracks, we were not allowed to go out. And this gave us a lot of hope. This was hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who was bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who cares? Who cares? The Russian, or the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew that that was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at what point did you leave the factory, the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bombing was going so often that we didn't go to the factory anymore, a few days. And the German round us up, and they made a speech that-- it's not the Russians. I don't know who was. That the Americans are coming, and we-- if they come in, we should tell them that they were good to us. You understand? And we promised anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were German soldiers, or the people who owned-- the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS. The SS. And if we will hear the siren go up a certain thing, then we are free. That's what they said. Everybody was happy. We were staying in, hopeful, dancing. And a few hours later, they round us up again, at cell appel, that they have-- new-- they told them that they have to evacuate the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: New orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: New orders to evacuate the camp. And we were evacuated in two hours. And we were going to the death march for five weeks, from one forest to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went? Everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The hospital stayed. Just the hospital. And they were liberated the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that you mention hospital, I'd like to just go back. Were you ever sick--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just undernourished. The doctor, we had a doctor. A very nice doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: A German doctor or a Jewish doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one of us. One of us. A very nice doctor. He had no medicine, but he took me into the hospital just to rest, so I don't have to go to work. And he says, little girl, the only thing you need is a lot of soup. But I rested in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Czech doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So treatment in the hospital was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zero.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the hospital? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. He had nurses, two. They helped the sick people. Oh. He made operations with a knife, and he burned the knife on a candle. And a girl, he was cutting by the throat. She had something. So that's how he operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: No anesthesia? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The nurses were Jewish too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of surgery did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the neck, she had some growth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So let's go back to the death march. Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just running from the liberators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know if you were going east, west, north, south?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant, who were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS went.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men or women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men. And you went for five weeks? How did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a few days, they were serving potatoes. And we were always camping close to a-- not a river. A little--

speaker: interviewer
text: A stream.

speaker: interviewee
text: A stream. So water, we had. We ate the grass. We ate the peel of the trees and the leaves. When there was nothing left, then they took us to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long would you camp at a stream? For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pass villages and towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the population see you, say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they help? What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. Nobody asked them. But sometime, they threw something, a piece of bread, a candy. I catched a candy from the population, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No men were marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the soldiers were all men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you were in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It has to be around Leipzig. How much could we walk? How much could we walk?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say it was five--

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weeks? Anybody die on route?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We just left them. Didn't even shot them. They just fell down. Whoever fell down, stayed. They tramped on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. I have to tell you this. I find dead animals were on the way, too, on the side. We were walking on the [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: On the what?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the road. [HEBREW] [LAUGHS] is Hebrew. And on the side were dead animals. Why were dead animals? I have no idea. But we grabbed those dead animals and ate the meat, as it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Raw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raw. And what I got was just a bone, a bone that long. And I was sucking on this bone for days, a few days. The marrow. And I have no idea if it was an animal. It could be a person, too. I just find the bone. But a few days, it kept me alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you know that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I know the war is over? I ran away from this place. We were in a-- in a forest. I was so weak, that I lie on the floor, and I say, this is the end. I'm never going to get up from here. I was hallucinating. And somehow, I said, no, I give one more try. And I run away. I hear that a group of people are planning to run away. And I was listening in to them, and they were going on their legs, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were crawling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crawling. And I crawled after them, and that's how I got out from this place and came to a farm, and went with the pigs, where the pigs ate. I grabbed a piece of this food. It was delicious. This was the first food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: All alone. The other people went back, because the Germans were shooting. They saw something is going on. The Germans were shooting, and they got scared. They went back. And I said, I'm not going back. I waited for a while, and then I crawled out of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of farm was it? Did anybody see you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From the window, somebody was watching me. And they saw that I eat the pig food, so they throw down a sandwich and close the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I ate the sandwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. Never. No, no. I didn't run away to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was walking the fields. And in the fields, I had food. It was-- They had some-- it was sweet, a green stuff, and maybe it was sugar canes or something. And I lived on this. It was pretty good. I was sucking on it for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was walking around by myself, and then I saw a town that the people, the Germans, were running away from the Russians. The Russians were attacking the town, and they were running away, and for a while, I went with them. And then I catched on, what am I doing? I don't want to run-- run away from the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was hiding in a barn. And I stay in this barn until the Russians came in. And I saw tanks and trucks. So this was the lib-- and they were so happy, singing, screaming, drinking. And I walked out of the barn, and this was the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the first person you saw when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that incident? Do you remember the soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long story. Do we have time for this story?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have time, yes. I'll stop you when we change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I find people, Russian people who came to Germany, and not in the camps. They were working for farmers. Because the Germans went away to war, the young men, so they needed somebody to work in the fields. So they had Russian people working for them in the fields. And I go with this group together, and they say they're going to Odessa. And this was some three couples, and they had children, too, babies. And they took wagons and horses-- the Germans-- and packed stuff on it, and we are going to Odessa. And I went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans or the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. I'm going with them. And I worked with them for a while, and they fed me, and I slept with them. And then, a Russian officer stopped them and ask, who are you. And they said who they are. And he started to scream, I know who you are. You didn't want to go to the Russian army, so you volunteered for Germany. I'm going to get you now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was people like this. And he took away the men. All the men are coming with us, and all the horses, and the women and the children can stay here. A middle of the street, a middle of nowhere. And the women started to scream, what will we do. We don't have men, and we don't have horses. How will we get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were crying, and I think for myself, what am I doing. They're none of my business. Let them cry. I walked away. I walked away. And I don't know if I can talk about this, but a Russian soldier-- I asked for direction, and he almost raped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I fainted, under him, and he went away. And this wasn't the one. Came another one. And this was in Dresden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, in the city of Dresden. Dresden is a city was burned down to the ground. They have a book about it. And when I walked away from the Russian soldiers, I met British officers. And they were just walking-- it wasn't a street, because it was all ashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just walking like you'd walk on Fifth Avenue. And one of them spoke German, and he asks me who I am. And I said to them who I am, [NON-ENGLISH], because we were called [NON-ENGLISH]. Where you been? And I told them where I'd been. So the British soldiers asked, we can help you. We can take you [BACKGROUND NOISE] to our camp where you will be taken care of, and nurses will take care of you. And he used the word 'camp' instead of Red Cross, and I hear the word 'camp.' No more camp for me, and I didn't go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a big mistake, because they had set up a place for people like me. And this was a very big mistake. I just didn't know it. Just because he used the word 'camp.'

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm going to change the tape.
